title: Listening - Part 4: Trying Extreme Sports
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people talking about their experiences of taking up an extreme sport in their later years

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d spent forty years as an accountant, and [21]I took up skydiving primarily to prove to myself that I hadn't lost my sense of adventure as I got older[/21]. I didn't want to become predictable. [26]The most unexpected benefit has been the massive boost in my everyday confidence[/26]. After you’ve jumped out of a plane at ten thousand feet, a difficult board meeting feels like a walk in the park. It’s given me a sense of perspective that has made me much more decisive and less prone to over-thinking minor problems. || Speaker 2: I’m a grandmother of three, and [22]I started white-water rafting because I wanted to find an activity that I could actually enjoy with my teenage grandsons[/22]. I didn't want to be the one just watching from the bank. It was terrifying but exhilarating. [27]What I found most difficult was the physical toll it took on my body for days afterward[/27]. Every muscle ached, and I realized I needed to significantly improve my core strength if I wanted to keep up. It’s been a wake-up call for my fitness, and I’ve actually started going to the gym for the first time in my life. || Speaker 3: I’ve always been terrified of the ocean, so [23]I took up scuba diving as a way to confront my deepest fear and reclaim my sense of bravery[/23]. I wanted to stop being afraid. [28]The most rewarding aspect has been the profound sense of peace and stillness I feel underwater[/28]. It’s like a form of meditation. I never realized how much the noise of the world was affecting me until I was down there in the silence. It’s become my ultimate escape from the stress of my urban life, and I find myself craving that quietude every weekend. || Speaker 4: I’m a retired teacher, and [24]I started rock climbing because I was looking for a hobby that would provide both a physical and a mental challenge[/24]. I didn't want a passive retirement. It’s like solving a puzzle with your whole body. [29]The biggest hurdle for me has been the initial skepticism and concern from my family[/29]. They think I’m being reckless at sixty-five, and I’ve had to spend a lot of time reassuring them that I’m being safe. It’s taught me a lot about standing my ground and prioritizing my own happiness over other people's expectations. || Speaker 5: I’d always been a bit of a workaholic, so [25]I took up mountain biking because I needed a hobby that was intense enough to force me to stop thinking about my job[/25]. I needed a total mental reset. [30]The most significant outcome has been the discovery of a vibrant and supportive community of fellow riders[/30]. We meet up every Sunday, and it’s given me a whole new social life that is completely separate from my professional identity. It’s been a wonderful way to broaden my horizons and meet people from all walks of life who share the same passion.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to try an extreme sport.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted to prove he hadn't lost his sense of adventure.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted an activity to enjoy with her teenage grandsons.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to confront his deepest fear of the ocean.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 was looking for a hobby that provided a physical and mental challenge.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 needed a hobby intense enough to stop him thinking about work.

options.A: to recover from a recent health scare
options.B: to find a new way to stay intellectually active
options.C: to bond with younger family members
options.D: to prepare for a professional competition
options.F: to raise money for a local charity

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of their experience.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a massive boost in everyday confidence.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions the physical toll and muscle aches afterward.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 highlights the profound sense of peace and stillness underwater.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions the initial skepticism and concern from her family.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 highlights the discovery of a supportive community of fellow riders.

options.A: a realization that they were not suited to the sport
options.B: an increased sense of inner tranquility
options.C: the discovery of a lucrative new business idea
options.D: a positive impact on their professional self-esteem
options.F: the surprising physical after-effects of the activity
options.G: having to deal with the disapproval of others
options.H: the benefit of a new social support group
